title: ProGRank: Probe-Gradient Reranking to Defend Dense-Retriever RAG from Corpus Poisoning
abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) improves the reliability of large language model applications by grounding generation in retrieved evidence, but it also introduces a new attack surface: corpus poisoning. In this setting, an adversary injects or edits passages so that they are ranked into the Top-$K$ results for target queries and then affect downstream generation. Existing defences against corpus poisoning often rely on content filtering, auxiliary models, or generator-side reasoning, which can make deployment more difficult. We propose ProGRank, a post hoc, training-free retriever-side defence for dense-retriever RAG. ProGRank stress-tests each query--passage pair under mild randomized perturbations and extracts probe gradients from a small fixed parameter subset of the retriever. From these signals, it derives two instability signals, representational consistency and dispersion risk, and combines them with a score gate in a reranking step. ProGRank preserves the original passage content, requires no retraining, and also supports a surrogate-based variant when the deployed retriever is unavailable. Extensive experiments across three datasets, three dense retriever backbones, representative corpus poisoning attacks, and both retrieval-stage and end-to-end settings show that ProGRank provides stronger defence performance and a favorable robustness--utility trade-off. It also remains competitive under adaptive evasive attacks.
intro:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) improves the reliability of large language model (LLM) applications by grounding generation in externally retrieved evidence [cite]. 
In practical deployments, the retrieval corpus is often assembled from web-scale or user-contributed sources rather than a fully curated collection, making the corpus an additional trust boundary for the RAG pipeline [cite]. 
Modern RAG systems commonly rely on neural dense retrievers that rank passages by embedding similarity and pass the Top-K passages to the generator [cite].

Among the emerging risks, corpus poisoning is particularly concerning. In this setting, an adversary injects or edits a small number of passages so that they are ranked into the Top-K results for target queries and subsequently steer generation through poisoned evidence [cite]. 
This threat model covers representative attack families, including retrieval-optimized poisoning [cite], joint optimization with retrieval and generation objectives [cite], trigger-based poisoning that activates under specific queries [cite], and misleading-evidence attacks that induce unfaithful or harmful generations [cite]. 
Our goal is to reduce poisoned Top-K exposure at retrieval time, thereby lowering downstream attack success, without modifying the generator or retraining the retriever.

Existing defences can be grouped by the stage at which they intervene. Generation-time defences change how retrieved evidence is consumed during decoding, for example via aggregation schemes [cite],

attention constraints [cite], or activation-based analysis [cite]. 

System-level approaches aim to identify and remove poisoned sources in the corpus, which often requires corpus-wide access and offline processing [cite]. 
Retriever-side defences instead act on the ranking itself by reducing the chance that poisoned passages enter the Top-K set. However, existing poisoning-mitigation methods at or around retrieval often rely on auxiliary detectors or content-based filtering [cite], dedicated reranking procedures [cite], or hybrid pipelines that invoke language-model reasoning beyond retrieval [cite], which can complicate deployment.

 
 < g r a p h i c s >
 
 Comparison between the undefended ranking pipeline and our proposed probe-gradient reranking. ProGRank extracts two sensitivity-based penalties, a consistency penalty and a saturated risk penalty, and fuses them with a score gate to obtain the defended reranking score, thereby suppressing poisoned passages and reducing poisoned Top-K exposure.

We propose ProGRank, a post hoc, training-free retriever-side defence for neural dense retrievers in RAG. As illustrated in Fig. <ref>,
ProGRank performs a lightweight stress test on each query–passage pair by computing probe gradients of the retriever similarity score with respect to a small fixed parameter subset, namely a LayerNorm module at layer L, under mild randomized perturbations of the retriever computation. 
From these probe gradients, we derive two complementary instability signals, representational consistency (Rep) and dispersion risk (DR), also denoted as the consistency penalty and the saturated risk penalty.
Unlike generic uncertainty-based reranking, ProGRank probes parameter-space sensitivity rather than only representation-space variability, and combines these signals with a score gate that focuses correction on the high-score region that determines Top-K membership.
The key intuition is that optimization-driven poisons can concentrate retrievability on a small set of perturbation-sensitive matching signals, which makes them more likely to yield unstable gradient responses under stochastic perturbations than benign passages. 
We do not assume that all poisoned passages are intrinsically unstable, rather, we treat such instability as a useful reranking signal for poisoning mitigation.
ProGRank can be used in a white-box setting with direct access to the retriever, and it also admits a surrogate-based variant for settings where the deployed retriever is unavailable.

We evaluate ProGRank across multiple datasets and dense retriever backbones [cite], under representative poisoning strategies [cite]. 
Overall, ProGRank reduces poisoned Top-K exposure while maintaining retrieval utility, and lowers downstream attack success.

 
Contributions.
Our contributions are three-fold: (1) We propose ProGRank, a post hoc, training-free retriever-side defence that reranks candidates using probe-gradient instability under randomized perturbations. (2) We introduce a score-gated penalty fusion mechanism that focuses robustness correction on the decision-critical high-score region. (3) We show across multiple datasets, retrievers, and poisoning attacks that ProGRank reduces poisoned Top-K exposure and improves downstream robustness, while remaining effective under the adaptive attack settings considered in this work.
related_work:
Corpus Poisoning Attacks.
Corpus poisoning attacks inject or modify a small number of passages so that targeted queries retrieve them and downstream generation is steered [cite]. Existing work studies direct passage injection against dense retrievers [cite], end-to-end poisoning objectives for RAG [cite], trigger-style poisoning [cite], and retrieval corruption through low-level perturbations [cite]; benchmarks further show that adaptive poisoning can degrade many defences [cite].

Comparison of representative defences against corpus poisoning in dense-retriever RAG. Columns highlight deployment properties relevant to our positioning.

ProGRank (ours) 

 
Defences Against Corpus Poisoning.
Existing defences span multiple stages of the RAG pipeline [cite]. Generation-time defences reduce the influence of retrieved evidence during decoding [cite], system-level approaches trace attacks back to poisoned texts for cleanup [cite], and retriever-side or retrieval-stage defences aim to prevent poisoned passages from entering the Top-K set, often via filtering or detector-style scoring [cite], dedicated reranking [cite], retrieval-stage content manipulation such as partitioning or masking [cite], or hybrid verification with additional language-model reasoning [cite].

 
Positioning.
ProGRank is a post hoc, training-free retriever-side defence that uses only retriever-derived signals, without auxiliary language-model inference, content filtering, or generator-side verification. Unlike filtering or hybrid verification methods [cite], it operates directly on query–passage signals from the retriever. Unlike approaches that alter retrieved content [cite], it preserves the original passage content and adjusts scores in a post hoc reranking step. Unlike dedicated reranking procedures [cite], it does not introduce a separate learned reranker or auxiliary model. ProGRank also admits a surrogate-based variant for settings where the deployed retriever is unavailable.
